text.title: Solo Lead Qualification — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that govern the four-dimensional ICE-style score (1-5 each, max 20) and the pass / soft-pass / decline thresholds.

rule.statement: Always score four dimensions: Budget-clarity, Decision-on-call, Timeline-realism, Contractor-trauma-history. Each on a 1-5 integer scale. Never score on fewer than four; never collapse to a single number without the per-dimension table.

rationale.source: Christensen JTBD / Sean Ellis ICE / HubSpot BANT retirement

rule.rationale: BANT collapsed four concerns into a binary qualify/disqualify, which forces an LLM to over-weight the loudest signal in the thread. Per-dimension scoring forces evidence per axis and exposes the trade-off (e.g. clear budget but unclear authority).

rule.statement: Every dimension score must cite at least one verbatim quote from the DM/email thread. A score without a citation is invalid and must be coerced to NULL.
rule.rationale: LLMs given a transcript will confabulate budgets, role titles, and timelines that were never stated. Verbatim grounding is the only reliable falsifier.

rule.statement: Total ≥ 14 → BOOK discovery call. Total ∈ [10,13] → SOFT-PASS, send a single calibration question first. Total < 10 → POLITE-DECLINE with templated reply. Never proceed to paid discovery / proposal work below 14.

rationale.source: Empirical — Brennan Dunn (Double Your Freelancing) close-rate cohort

rule.rationale: In freelancer cohorts tracking discovery-to-paid conversion, leads scoring < 14 on equivalent multi-axis rubrics close at < 8%, but consume 2.4× the proposal time of qualified leads. The threshold is a margin-defense rule, not a snobbery rule.

rule.statement: If the prospect's quoted or implied budget is below the operator's rate-floor (loaded from `solo-rate-floor-calculator`), then Budget-clarity is automatically forced to 1, regardless of how unambiguous the budget statement is. A clearly-stated bad budget is still a bad budget.
rule.rationale: The most common LLM scoring mistake is rewarding "they were upfront about $2k" with a 5 on Budget-clarity. Upfront and unaffordable is still unaffordable. Coupling to the operator's floor turns clarity into qualified-clarity.

rule.statement: Contractor-trauma-history is scored asymmetrically: a clean history scores 5, one bad prior engagement scores 3, two or more bad priors score 1. Score CANNOT exceed 3 if the thread contains any of the marker phrases: "last developer disappeared", "previous freelancer", "burned by", "another agency", "rescue", "salvage". A 1 here vetoes the total — push to POLITE-DECLINE regardless of other dimensions.

rationale.source: Patrick McKenzie / Jonathan Stark consulting bibliography

rule.rationale: Scarred prospects re-enact the trauma. They under-pay, micro-manage, and dispute scope. The asymmetric veto exists because the other three dimensions cannot compensate: a 5-5-5-1 lead is statistically worse than a 3-3-3-3 lead.

example.title: Budget-clarity anchors
example.code: 5 — exact number stated AND ≥ rate-floor: "Budget is $8k for the build." 4 — range stated AND midpoint ≥ floor: "Somewhere between $5k and $10k." 3 — unit stated, total unclear: "$80/hr is what we paid last guy." 2 — vague but positive: "We have budget approved." 1 — silent OR explicitly below floor: "What's the cheapest you'd go?"
example.note: r1+r4: clarity AND ≥ floor required for 4-5. Below-floor caps at 1.

example.title: Decision-on-call anchors
example.code: 5 — prospect is founder/owner, will be on call alone. 4 — decision-maker + one technical contributor. 3 — middle manager who reports decision; cycle 1-2 weeks. 2 — committee implied: "I'll loop in my partner / CTO / wife." 1 — gatekeeper: "I'm researching for my boss / client."
example.note: r2: must cite the quote that triggered the score.

example.title: Timeline-realism anchors
example.code: 5 — start in 2-6 weeks, duration matches scope reality. 4 — start in 6-12 weeks, duration reasonable. 3 — "no rush, when you can". (Soft signal — risk of indefinite drift.) 2 — "we're flexible" with no anchor date. 1 — "by Friday" / "we needed this last month" / "yesterday".
example.note: Counter-intuitive: BOTH "yesterday" AND "no rush" score badly. The first scopes scope-creep; the second never closes.

example.title: Single-signal scoring (r1 violation)
example.code:
Lead said "budget is $20k". Scored 5/20 → BOOK.
Wrong: r1 demands 4 dimensions. Authority, timeline, trauma all unscored. Score is invalid; output must be rejected.
example.note: Illustrates r1 violation — the most common LLM shortcut.
